column4: Latinate verb "edify/edifien" long-naturalized via French, productive -y verb class; no independent hit but standard EModE religious vocabulary formation
effectual + productive -ly adverb suffix, fully naturalized Latinate adjective class
Latinate adjective, -ate suffix class productive since ME borrowings
ordinal numeral, native -eth ordinal suffix fully productive
genitive of "elect" (Latinate adjective used as noun), standard genitive formation
French-derived verb (en- + balm), attested loan pattern
en- + bold + -en(ed), fully productive native prefix/suffix pattern
en- + camp, productive native/French prefix pattern
en- + damage is a plausible but unattested formation; "damage" alone as verb is well attested, prefixed form cannot be independently confirmed
en- + danger, productive prefix on established French loan
en- + grave (to carve), native/French prefix pattern
strong past participle of engrave, native -en pattern
-est thou-form of enquire (French loan), fully productive verb class
en- + tangle, productive native prefix pattern
regular past tense of "entice", established French loan
entrance + plural, established French loan compound
espouse + -al + plural, established French loan family
-eth form of "establish", established French loan verb
regular past tense of "estrange", established French loan
Greek/Latin loanword with long medieval ecclesiastical pedigree, plural regular
Greek/Latin loanword, ME "evaungelist" attested, plural regular
native compound (even + tide), old Anglo-Saxon compounding pattern
native compound, same pattern as eventide
native compound (evil + doer), fully transparent and productive
native compound + -ness, transparent and productive (cf. favoureth below)
-eth form of "exact" (Latinate verb), productive verb class
confirmed in Geneva1599 Acts 19:13 "exorcistes", Tyndale Acts 19:13 "exorcistes", Wycliffe Acts 19:13 "exorsisists" — genuinely attested in three independent pre-1611 witnesses
Latinate noun, established loan, attested by 16th c.
genitive of native noun "eye", standard formation
native compound, transparent (cf. Rev 3:18 imagery), plausible formation
native compound, transparent, plausible Pauline coinage-style compound
native compound "eye-witness", transparent agent compound, productive pattern (cf. "ear-witness")
native compound (faint + hearted), transparent and productive
native compound (fallow + deer), transparent color-adjective + animal compound
plural of "fat" (noun, a vat), plain native word
comparative of "fat", regular native adjective inflection
superlative of "fat", regular native adjective inflection
plural of native noun "fear", plain vocabulary
regular past tense of "feast" (French loan verb), established
native compound (feeble + minded), transparent, cf. "-hearted" compounds
regular past tense of "feign" (French loan), established

superlative of "fell" (adj., cruel) is attested in EModE generally but not independently confirmed here; the adjective "fell" itself is well-attested (cf. Wycliffe/Chaucer), superlative form unverified
native compound, transparent Pauline-style "fellow-" compound (cf. gainsay compounding pattern confirmed in ME Reader)

no independent attestation for "ferret" naming the Lev 11:30 unclean creature; Geneva1599 reads "molle" (mole), Wycliffe reads "maldewerp" (mole); ME Reader glossary attests "wesele"/weasel as the native small-mammal term — ferret's presence here has no independent-corpus support
regular past tense of native "fetch", plain vocabulary
native compound (first + begotten), transparent, theologically established phrase pattern
native compound, fully transparent and productive
genitive of native "fisher" (agent noun fish + -er), standard
genitive of native "fool" (French loan by ME), standard formation
foolish + -ness, productive, cf. widely attested "-ishness" pattern
forbear (native) + -ance, productive Latinate/French nominalizer on native root
native strong past participle of "forbear", regular ablaut pattern
native compound past tense "fore-" + "knew", productive prefix
-est thou-form past tense of native "forgive", productive
plural of forgiveness, regular though rare
archaic conjunction "for so much (as)", attested compounding pattern in EModE legal/religious prose
genitive-apostrophe of a bare cardinal numeral is not a natural EModE construction; no independent ground found; likely a corrupted or mis-parsed form

no genuine independent attestation; the only apparent Essayes hit is OCR noise ("Viforum", part of a Latin phrase, not the English word); in the period "forum" functioned as a Latin proper place-name for Rome's forum, not yet a naturalized generic English common noun
regular past tense of native "foul", plain vocabulary
-edst thou-form past tense, productive (cf. -est pattern extended to past tense)
native compound numeral (four + score), well-established EModE counting idiom
native compound (free + will), transparent, established theological term
comparative of "fresh", regular native adjective inflection
genitive of native agent-noun "fuller" (full + -er), standard formation
regular past tense of native "further", plain vocabulary
-est thou-form of native "gad" (to wander), productive verb class

confirmed compounding pattern "gainsay/gainsaying" attested in Middle English Reader glossary ("ME. saie(n), seie(n); gainsay ... gainsaying, contradiction"); agent-noun "-er" extension is standard productive formation
native compound (gar + leek), old Anglo-Saxon plant-name compound
native compound (gazing + stock), transparent, cf. attested pattern "laughingstock"
Latinate/French verb "gender" (to beget), established loan, regular past tense
Greek/Latin loanword via French, long medieval pedigree
French/Latin loanword "geant", established by ME, plural regular
native (or Germanic-cognate) noun of uncertain deep etymology but attested in ME (Chaucer "gerl"), plain vocabulary
-eth form of native "give", productive and core verb class
-ing form of native "glister", productive; cf. widely known EModE usage
native "god" + French -esse suffix, established hybrid pattern by ME
genitive of native compound "goldsmith", standard formation
superlative of native "goodly", regular adjective inflection

transliteration of Hebrew term (Gen 6:14 "gopher wood"); as a technical hapax transliteration it cannot be checked against English word-formation or the local period corpora, which show no hit; no independent ground either way
genitive of native compound "godspell/gospel", standard formation
plural of "gourd" (French/Latin loanword), established by ME
